description: Interviewer behavior cases for the directive-based question writer. The opening check is scripted: a new-topic directive must yield a question referencing at least one concrete claim from the committed synthetic profile. The follow-up cases are judged by an LLM (smart slot) because 'chases the directive's subject' and 'leads the witness' cannot be string-matched; judge verdicts get a human spot-check per plans/decisions/evals-and-observability.md. The personalization case pins Phase 4a's question-personalization criterion: a why-this-company directive with a company brief must yield a question naming the company's actual product or domain. Every generated question and the closing turn additionally pass a deterministic voice tell-scan, and the leakage case checks that nothing from the prompt's style examples surfaces in output.

projects.name: openroster
projects.description: Open-source shift-planning library in Python with 2300 GitHub stars; maintainer since 2019.

projects.name: ferryline
projects.description: Hobby real-time ferry tracker for the Danube, built with Go and WebSockets.

quantified_claims: cutting data latency from 4 hours to 90 seconds
Mentored a team of 6 engineers through the company's first SOC 2 audit
saving an estimated 40000 EUR per year in demurrage fees
driving incident count down from 12 to 3 per quarter

directive.subject: a story about real ownership in their past work
directive.reason: ownership is what this role leans on hardest

quantified_claims: halved the nightly report run from 6 hours to 3

transcript.question: You halved the nightly report run at Quietwater Labs. Tell me about that.
transcript.answer: The reports kept overrunning into business hours, so I profiled the job and rewrote the slowest queries.

directive.subject: what came of the report-run rewrite
directive.reason: the story has no ending yet

personalization.brief: Brightmark Health builds appointment scheduling and triage software for rural clinics. Its backend keeps patient-facing scheduling reliable for practices with no IT staff of their own; downtime means missed appointments for real patients. Clinicians use the triage module to sort incoming calls by urgency, and the company has lately been extending it to text-message intake for patients without smartphones.

directive.subject: why this company, and whether they understand what it makes
directive.reason: the stories are done and motivation is still unprobed

transcript.question: You moved Helioscope's ingestion pipeline from nightly batches to streaming. Tell me about that.
transcript.answer: We were on nightly batches and clients kept complaining that dashboards were stale, so I designed a streaming pipeline on Kafka and led the rollout across our Python consumers.

transcript.question: You moved Helioscope's ingestion pipeline from nightly batches to streaming. Tell me about that.
transcript.answer: We were on nightly batches and clients kept complaining that dashboards were stale, so I designed a streaming pipeline on Kafka and led the rollout across our Python consumers.

transcript.question: And once the streaming pipeline was live, what came of it?
transcript.answer: Dashboard latency went from four hours to about ninety seconds, and the stale-data complaints stopped almost entirely.

transcript.question: You moved Helioscope's ingestion pipeline from nightly batches to streaming. Tell me about that.
transcript.answer: We were on nightly batches and clients kept complaining that dashboards were stale, so I designed a streaming pipeline on Kafka and led the rollout across our Python consumers.

directive.subject: what came of the streaming migration for the clients and the business
directive.reason: the story ends at the rollout; the outcome is never stated

transcript.question: Tell me about the berth scheduling work at Dunav Logistics.
transcript.answer: Ships kept idling outside the port because berth assignments were planned by hand on a spreadsheet, so I built an optimizer that planned the assignments a week ahead and worked with the operations team until they trusted its plans.

directive.subject: how the berth optimizer worked out once the operations team ran with it
directive.reason: the story stops at adoption; what changed for the port is never said

transcript.question: You moved Helioscope's ingestion pipeline from nightly batches to streaming. Tell me about that.
transcript.answer: We were on nightly batches and clients kept complaining that dashboards were stale. I led the move to Kafka streaming, and after the rollout the dashboards updated much faster and the complaints mostly stopped.

directive.subject: how much faster the dashboards actually got
directive.reason: the outcome is stated but nothing measurable says how big it was

transcript.question: Tell me about the SOC 2 audit at Helioscope.
transcript.answer: The company had never been audited before and we needed SOC 2 to close enterprise deals, so the team spent two quarters getting controls in place and we passed on the first try.

directive.subject: what they personally did during the two audit quarters
directive.reason: the answer is all 'the team'; their own part never appears

directive.subject: the ferryline side project and what building it on their own looked like
directive.reason: their professional work is well covered; the side project shows how they build when nobody assigns the work

transcript.question: You moved Helioscope's ingestion pipeline from nightly batches to streaming. Tell me about that.
transcript.answer: Clients were churning over stale dashboards, so I proposed the streaming rebuild, led two engineers through it, and we took latency from 4 hours to 90 seconds.

transcript.question: What was the hardest call during that rollout?
transcript.answer: Holding the cutover a week when a partition-skew bug showed up, even with sales pushing to announce. We cut over clean and the client renewed.

cv_verbatim_substrings: real-time ferry tracker for the danube
